coredata.dc:title: Construction and application of targeted drug delivery system based on hyaluronic acid and heparin functionalised carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Ma, Lan
Zhang, Boye
Zhang, Yixia
Li, Xiaoning
Wang, Tao
Zhang, Wendong
Li, Yi
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: Thrombosis is a main complication of cancer. It can increase the mortality of cancer patients. Therefore, the anticoagulant heparin (Hep) as an adjuvant therapy was introduced to the drug delivery system based on doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX)-carbon dots (CDs)-Hyaluronic acid (HA), which obviously enhanced the blood compatibility of the system. Drug release process of the CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system was dual-responsive by HA and pH value. Results of in vitro MTT and scratch tests demonstrated that the drug delivery system could targetedly inhibit growth and migration of cancerous cells. In addition, the system allows visual tracking of the drug based on fluorescence of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heparinized carbon dots
Targeted drug delivery system
Cytotoxicity
Cell migration
Drug tracking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro release of DOX from CDs-HA-Hep/Dox system
Drug loading efficiency and in vitro release

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs (scale bar: 50 nm). (B) Particle size histograms of CDs. (C) Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths (inset: photographs of CDs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs-HA-Hep and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems. (E) FTIR spectra of CDs, CDs-HA-Hep and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems. (F) Zeta potential values of CDs, CDs-HA-Hep and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems at pH 7.4.
Drug release curves of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX under different conditions (obtained by monitoring the absorbance of DOX at 480 nm).
Hemolysis rates of CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX.

Cytotoxicities of CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX at different concentrations to MGC-803 cells for 24 h (A) and 48 h (B). Cytotoxicities of CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX at different concentrations to GES cells for 24 h (C) and 48 h (D).

(A) Images of scratch assay before and after 12 h/24 h incubation of MGC-803 cells with DOX (100 μg/mL), CDs-HA-Hep/DOX (10 μg/mL) and control group. Scale bars: 10 μm. (B) Cell mobility of MGC-803 cells treated with DOX, CDs-HA-Hep/DOX and control group for 24 h.

In vitro images of MGC-803 cells (A) and GES cells (B) treated with CDs-HA-Hep/DOX (10 μg/mL) for 6 h. Scale bars: 20 μm. (C) Normalization relative pixel intensities of the fluorescent images at different channels.
Synthesis, functionalization and release of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system.

head.ce:title: Construction and application of targeted drug delivery system based on hyaluronic acid and heparin functionalised carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Advanced Transducers and Intelligent Control System of the Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thrombosis is a main complication of cancer. It can increase the mortality of cancer patients. Therefore, the anticoagulant heparin (Hep) as an adjuvant therapy was introduced to the drug delivery system based on doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX)-carbon dots (CDs)-Hyaluronic acid (HA), which obviously enhanced the blood compatibility of the system. Drug release process of the CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system was dual-responsive by HA and pH value. Results of in vitro MTT and scratch tests demonstrated that the drug delivery system could targetedly inhibit growth and migration of cancerous cells. In addition, the system allows visual tracking of the drug based on fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemotherapy is a traditional cancer treatment. Its application is limited by its disadvantages such as low solubility, poor targeting, fast clearance, limited efficacy and serious side effects [1]. To solve these problems, various multi-functional drug delivery systems have been constructed [2]. They generally include carrier, drug, targeted molecule, responsive group, compatibility coating and so on [3]. However, hemocompatibility is rarely considered in these systems, although it is an important factor of chemotherapy.

Malignant tumors usually accompany the occurrence of hemostasis disorders that are mainly in a hypercoagulable state or a thrombophilic state [4–7]. Thrombus especially has a high incidence in terminal cancer. It is more likely to occur in the case of traditional chemotherapy and surgery. Hypercoagulability is caused by the expression of various coagulants known to play a positive role in the growth and metastasis of tumors [8,9]. In addition, nano-drug delivery system itself can cause blood clotting [10]. Therefore, anticoagulant heparin (Hep) has been used to modify the drug delivery system to improve hemocompatibility and reduce the risk of thrombus [11]. This not only improves thrombolytic state of tumor patients, but also inhibits the growth and metastasis of tumor cells caused by coagulation and platelet aggregation.

As a natural substance in animals, Hep has many advantages, such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, biodegradability, low immunogenicity, good water solubility and rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [12,13]. Hep could inhibit angiogenesis by binding to vascular endothelial growth factor, fibroblast growth factor and other growth factors [14,15]. In addition, it has been demonstrated that Hep can inhibit the interaction between tumor cells and platelets, thereby inhibiting tumor metastasis [16,17].

In this study, carbon dots were chosen as carriers because of their small particle size, high light stability, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, rich surface functional groups and other advantages [18–20]. As a glycoprotein, CD44 is overexpressed on the cell membrane of many tumors. Thus, it could be considered as a biomarker. As a primary receptor of CD44, hyaluronic acid (HA) has been used to construct CD44-HA specific affinity to realize cancer targeting [21–23]. In addition, HA receptor-mediated endocytosis pathway could make the drug enter cells more effectively [24,25].

In the present study, a CDs-HA-Hep/DOX multi-functional drug delivery system with HA-responsive was developed as shown in Scheme 1 . Amino-rich CDs was prepared by hydrothermal polymerization. HA and Hep were modified on the surface of CDs by EDC/NHS reaction. Then DOX was loaded by electrostatic binding. In vitro blood compatibility, drug release curve and cell viability were evaluated. The CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system exhibited good blood compatibility with active targeting and release. It also showed great potential in real-time imaging and monitoring of cell behavior during tumor treatment.

Glucose, glycol, polyethyleneimine (PEI), hyaluronic acid (HA), hyaluronidase (HAase), heparin sodium (Hep), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3- ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) were obtained from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), dialysis bags MD44 (MWCO 1000) and MD34 (MWCO 3500) and doxorubicin (DOX) were purchased from Shanghai Chuang Sai Science and Technology Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DMEM medium, Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 culture medium and all other chemicals used for cell culture were purchased from Mike Ward Biology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade.

First, 12.5 mL glycol and 12.5 mL deionized water were fully mixed to form a transparent solution. Then 0.5 g glucose was added to the mixture and slowly stirred until completely dissolved. After that, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL teflon lined autoclave for 3 h at 180℃ and then cooled to room temperature. Finally, 1 mL PEI solution was added into the teflon lined autoclave to react 3 h under 80℃. The solution was then filtered through syringe filter (pore size 0.22 μm) and solid particles were removed when naturally cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged (10,000 r/min, 10 min) to obtain pure CDs. Brown solution of CDs was maintained at 4 °C for subsequent testing.

HA and Hep were immobilized on the surface of CDs through carboxyl-amino condensation reaction to form CDs-HA-Hep complex. Firstly, 0.1 g HA was activated by 0.05 g NHS and 0.1 g EDC in 2 mL MES buffer (pH 5.6) for 15 min. Activated HA solution was then added to CDs solution (8 mL) with gentle agitation to form CDs-HA. In the same way, Hep (0.01 g) was activated and modified to the surface of CDs-HA to obtain CDs-HA-Hep complex. Finally, CDs-HA-Hep solution was purified to removed unreacted CDs, HA and Hep by centrifugation at 10,000 r/min for 20 min and dialyzed for 6 h.

To obtain CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system, DOX solutions at different concentrations were mixed with CDs-HA-Hep solution (10 mL) at pH 7.4. The filtrate was purified by dialysis against deionized water (MWCO 1000 Da) for 12 h to remove free DOX. The obtained CDs-HA-Hep/DOX solution was maintained at 4 °C in the dark. Drug loading efficiency was calculated according to the DOX calibration curve (Fig. S1) from measurement of absorbance of DOX at 480 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release of DOX from CDs-HA-Hep/Dox system
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-HA-Hep/DOX solution (10 mL) was added into the dialysis bag and dispersed into 50 mL buffer with different pH values. Then 3 mL solution in the dialysis bag was extracted regularly. The whole release process was stirred in a 37 °C water bath. Release amount and release curves of DOX were determined based on absorbance at 480 nm with a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Blood compatibilities of CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX were determined by hemolysis test. Fresh human blood of 2 mL from healthy volunteer was dissolved in 2.5 mL normal saline. Then CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX (0.1 mg/mL) solutions were placed in normal saline and stored at 37 °C for 30 min, respectively. CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX solutions (100 μL) were then added to diluted blood (0.2 mL) and kept at 37 °C for 3 h, respectively. Subsequently, the above solutions were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 15 min. Absorbance of the supernatant was then measured at wavelength of 545 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. Hemolysis degree was calculated as follows:
Hemolysis rate = (A sample -A negative) / (A positive - A negative) × 100 %,
where A sample, A negative and A positive denoted absorbance of the sample, negative control and positive control, respectively.

Cell viability was determined by MTT assay. MGC-803 and GES cells were inoculated onto 96-well culture plate at a seeding density of 2 × 104 cells per well and then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a humidified environment with 5 % CO2. Culture medium of the orifice was removed and fresh culture medium was added. CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX solutions with different concentrations (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μg/mL) were added into culture wells and incubated for 24 h and 48 h. After incubation, cells were washed with PBS three times. All cell culture media of 96-well plate were replaced with fresh culture media. MTT (10 μL, 5 mg/mL) was then added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Finally, 100 μL DMSO solution was added to fully dissolve the bluish violet crystal and the cell viability was estimated based on absorbance at 450 nm using a Microplate Reader.

In order to study the metastatic ability of cancer cells, scratch assay was carried out. Succinctly, the cell layer was scratched with the tip of a sterile straw when MGC-803 cells (cultured in 12-well plate) reached near confluence. Cells were then incubated with DOX or CDs-HA-Hep/DOX (60 μL) for fixed hours (0, 6, 12, 24 h) and photographed for observation. Untreated cells were used as control.

Cell imaging was performed to study the labeling and phagocytosis of normal cells and cancer cells. First, cells were seeded into a 24-well plate containing a cover slip and incubated for 24 h. When adherent cells reached 80 % confluency, culture medium containing CDs, DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX was used to replace the old culture medium and cells were cultured for 6 h. Subsequently, cells were washed with PBS three times, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (1 mL) for 15 min and observed under a fluorescence microscope.

CDs were well-dispersed and spherical with an average particle size of 3.9 nm (Fig. 1 A-1B). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1C, CDs emitted intense green fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp. The fluorescence emission peak exhibited obvious red-shift with increasing excitation wavelength. The excitation-dependent emission feature might be due to band gap of CDs with different surface states. Due to π→π* transition of CC band and n→π* transition of CO [26,27], CDs showed special absorption peaks at 220 nm and 280 nm (Fig. 1D).

Hydration radius, UV–vis absorption, FTIR spectra and zeta potential were used to verify effective functionalization of CDs and synthesis of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system. Hydration radius values of CDs, CDs-HA-Hep and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems were gradually increased, corresponding to average sizes of 117.1 nm (0.012 PDI), 262 nm (0.176 PDI) and 275 nm (0.237 PDI), respectively (Fig. S2). After functionalization of HA and Hep (Fig. 2 D), CDs-HA-Hep showed a new peak between 220 nm and 280 nm due to π−π* transition of aromatic ring structure, indicating that amide reaction maybe take place [28]. Meanwhile, due to action of DOX, CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system showed characteristic peak at 480 nm [19]. Scissor vibration of -NH2 (1651 cm−1) and stretching vibration of -NH2 and −OH (3287 cm−1) were observed in FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1E), indicating that the surface of CDs was rich in -NH2 groups [29]. The peak at 1561 cm−1 further proved the occurrence of amide reaction between CDs with amino group and HA/Hep with carboxyl group [30]. As shown in Fig. 1f, the zeta potential of CDs was 12 mV due to abundant amino groups of the surface. The potential value changed to −18 mV when CDs was combined with HA/Hep because of carboxyl and other negative groups on Hep/HA molecules. By comparison, the surface potential (−16 mV) of CDs-HA-Hep increased after DOX was loaded, thus verifying the electrostatic interaction between them.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading efficiency and in vitro release
ce:section.ce:para: Stimulus-response drug release phenomenon of the drug delivery system was further evaluated. The drug loading efficiency of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system was calculated to be 67 %, which was higher than that of other reported similar drug delivery system [7,31,32]. The release effect was evaluated under four different conditions (Fig. 2). Without HAase, there were basically no release both at pH 7.4 and pH 5.6 within 72 h. When the HAase was present, an obvious release with the efficiency of 66 % was observed only at pH 5.6. Therefore, the drug release process was dual-responsive by HA and pH value. The drug delivery system exhibited excellent tumor targeting ability: high release efficiency under tumor microenvironment (pH 5.6, HAase) and almost no release under normal physiological condition (pH 7.4, without HAase). So, the drug delivery system should be very stable in blood circulation and have a very small osmotic quantity of drug in the normal tissue to reduce side effect [33]. When the drug delivery system arrived at the tumor site that is rich in receptor CD44, specific binding within targeted cells by swallowing function will occur. HA would be specially degraded by HAase in the cancerous cell. Stimulus-response of drug release will then occur.

Blood compatibility test is an important part of biocompatibility test. It can be used to evaluate compatibility between biological materials and blood. Compared to hemolysis rates of CDs or DOX, the hemolysis rate of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX decreased by about half (Fig. 3 ). This rate has a better satisfaction for hemolysis performance of biomedical materials [34,35]. Measurement results indicated that modification of Hep indeed improved blood compatibility. This may affect the coagulation process and reduce thrombolytic status in patients with malignant tumors.

Cytotoxicities of CDs and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems were studied using MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 4 , viabilities of both MGC-803 and GES cells after incubation for 24 h and 48 h with CDs were higher than 85 %, indicating that CDs had low toxicity and side effects. Viabilities of MGC-803 and GES cells incubated with free DOX and CDs-HA-Hep/DOX systems for 24 h or 48 h were compared. For cancerous cells MGC-803, a dosage-dependent death was observed. The inhibition effect of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system was more obvious than that of free DOX. Especially, cell viability was reduced to be below 20 % after 48 h of incubation when the concentration of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX was higher than 16 μg/mL. However, the cell viability was almost stable at 38 % even when free DOX concentration increased from 14 μg/mL to 20 μg/mL followed by incubation for 48 h. This might be due to the fact that heparin could alter the coagulation process and thus inhibit tumor cells growth. For normal cells GES, the effect of dosage of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system was weak compared to that of free DOX. Cell viability was almost above 80 % even at high concentration, indicating that cytotoxicity of the CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system to normal cells significantly decreased. The above results indicate that the CDs-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system can target cancer cells while effectively reducing the growth of cancer cells and having low toxic side effects on normal cells.

To further investigate the efficacy of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX delivery system compared to DOX, the maximum inhibitory concentration (IC50) of them were calculated (Fig. S3). IC50 values of free DOX for 24 h and 48 h were 10.29 μg/mL and 4.85 μg/mL, respectively. While IC50 values of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system for 24 h and 48 h were 4.23 μg/mL and 6.66 μg/mL, respectively. Obviously, when CDs-HA-Hep/DOX was incubated with MGC-803 for 24 h (Fig. S3b), drug sensitivity and semi-inhibitory concentration were both better than those for 48 h. They were also better than those of DOX and MGC-803 for 24 h or 48 h (Fig.S3a). However, the final efficacy of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX incubated with MGC-803 cells for 48 h was significantly better than that for 24 h. It was also better than the incubation with DOX for 24 h or 48 h.

The ability of cell movement and migration was studied by in vitro scratch test. The growth of cells after formation of artificial gap on the surface of cell monolayer was observed and evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 , scratches without drug treatment had obvious migration at 12 h. They basically fused after 24 h, with a mobility of 75 %. The rule of scratches treated by DOX was similar to that of the control group, with a mobility of 48 %. On the contrary, after CD-HA-Hep/DOX treatment, the migration ability of cells was significantly inhibited. Especially, the migration rate was only 22 % at 24 h. These results indicated that the CD-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system had a good inhibitory effect on cell activity and mobility, which was the key to prevent cancer metastasis. These results might also be due to the fact that the introduction of heparin inhibited the interaction between tumor cells and platelets, thereby inhibiting cell metastasis, consistent with previous reports [11,13].

Cell imaging and drug tracking abilities of the CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system also have been evaluated. Results are shown in Fig. 6 . After MGC-803 cells were cultivated with CDS-HA-Hep/DOX system, strong fluorescence signal lighted by CDs and DOX in green and red channels was observed. This showed that the CDS-HA-Hep/DOX system was internalized effectively into MGC-803 cells. It might be related to fast metabolism and absorption of cancer cells [36]. Conversely, GES treated CD-HA-Hep/DOX emitted very weak fluorescence in both green and red channels. Comparison results also demonstrated cancer cell targeting ability of the drug delivery system. In addition, fluorescent imaging results indicated that the drug delivery system had potential of visual drug tracking.

In the present study, an HA-responsive drug delivery system was constructed based on heparinized CDs. Hep obviously enhanced blood compatibility of the drug delivery system. Drug loading rate of CDs-HA-Hep/DOX system reached 67 %. Its release rate reached 66 % under double trigger of acidic environment and hyaluronic acid. Such CDs-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system could target cancerous cells and efficiently inhibit the growth and migration of cancer cells while showing reduced adverse side effects on normal cells. This drug delivery system also showed favorable cell imaging and drug tracking abilities. In conclusion, fluorescent CDs-HA-Hep/DOX drug delivery system has broad application prospects in the field of cancer treatment and visual drug tracking.

Qianqian Duan: Project administration, Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Lan Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Boye Zhang: Investigation. Yixia Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiaoning Li: Formal analysis. Tao Wang: Resources. Wendong Zhang: Supervision. Yi Li: Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Shengbo Sang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51505324, 51622507, 81901900, 81602506), and Basic Research Program of Shanxi for Youths (201701D221111), Scientific and Technologial Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (201802036).
